annotations.label: Logic

anchor.snippet: Logic is traditionally defined as the study of the laws of thought

annotations.note: An informal disciplinary definition with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Argument

anchor.snippet: An argument is a set of premises together with a conclusion

annotations.note: An informal definition of 'argument' that Mathlib does not formalize as such.

annotations.label: Formal logic

anchor.snippet: Formal logic (also known as symbolic logic ) is widely used in mathematical logic

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes concrete symbolic logics (first-order languages) but does not define 'formal logic' as a discipline.

annotations.label: Deductively valid argument

anchor.snippet: Formal logic is interested in deductively valid arguments

annotations.note: Semantic entailment (T ⊨ φ) captures validity, but Mathlib has no general 'valid argument' object.

annotations.label: Modus ponens rule

anchor.snippet: modus ponens is a rule of inference according to which all arguments of the form

annotations.note: Modus ponens is realized by core Lean function application, not by any named Mathlib inference-rule declaration.

annotations.label: Logical truth

anchor.snippet: A proposition is logically true if its truth depends only on the logical vocabulary used in it

annotations.note: Mathlib's ModelTheory has no tautology/validity predicate over formulas.

annotations.label: A logic (formal system)

anchor.snippet: a logic is a logical formal system

annotations.note: Mathlib provides concrete logical systems (a Language plus a Theory) but not a general notion of 'a logic'.

annotations.label: Informal logic

anchor.snippet: Informal logic uses non-formal criteria and standards to analyze and assess the correctness of arguments

annotations.note: Informal logic is outside the scope of formalized mathematics.

annotations.label: Birds-Tweety natural language argument

anchor.snippet: Birds fly. Tweety is a bird. Therefore, Tweety flies.

annotations.note: A natural-language defeasible-reasoning example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Non-deductive arguments

anchor.snippet: Non-deductive arguments make their conclusion probable but do not ensure that it is true

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize non-deductive/defeasible argumentation.

annotations.label: Inductive raven argument

anchor.snippet: all ravens I have seen so far are black

annotations.note: An inductive-reasoning example, unrelated to mathematical induction, with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Informal fallacies

anchor.snippet: Informal fallacies are incorrect arguments in which errors are present in the content and the context of the argument

annotations.note: Informal fallacies are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: False dilemma

anchor.snippet: A false dilemma , for example, involves an error of content by excluding viable options

annotations.note: A specific informal fallacy with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Premises and conclusions

anchor.snippet: Premises and conclusions are the basic parts of inferences or arguments

annotations.note: An informal characterization of argument parts with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Mars red planet support

anchor.snippet: Mars is a red planet

annotations.note: A natural-language example sentence, not a formal object.

annotations.label: Propositions as denotations

anchor.snippet: Propositions are the denotations of sentences and are usually seen as abstract objects

annotations.note: A philosophy-of-language view of propositions with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Psychologism

anchor.snippet: This position is known as psychologism

annotations.note: A philosophical position, not a mathematical concept.

annotations.label: Complex proposition

anchor.snippet: A complex proposition has other propositions as its constituents

annotations.note: Compound formulas built from subformulas via connectives are modeled by BoundedFormula's constructors.

annotations.label: Simple proposition

anchor.snippet: Simple propositions, on the other hand, do not have propositional parts

annotations.note: Atomic formulas (equal/rel constructors of BoundedFormula) correspond to simple propositions.

annotations.label: Predicate applied to singular term

anchor.snippet: can be formed by applying the predicate

annotations.note: Applying a relation/predicate to terms to form an atomic formula is captured by BoundedFormula.rel.

annotations.label: Logical truth

anchor.snippet: the truth is called a logical truth

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general 'logical truth'/validity predicate over its formula types.

annotations.label: Excluded-middle Mars proposition

anchor.snippet: either Mars is red or Mars is not red

annotations.note: An instance of the law of excluded middle, `em : ∀ p, p ∨ ¬p`.

annotations.label: Truth tables

anchor.snippet: Truth tables can be used to show how logical connectives work

annotations.note: Mathlib evaluates Boolean connectives via `decide`/`Bool` but does not formalize truth tables as objects.

annotations.label: Inference

anchor.snippet: An inference is the process of reasoning from these premises to the conclusion

annotations.note: The informal notion of inference-as-process has no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Sound argument

anchor.snippet: a sound argument is an argument that is both correct and has only true premises

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize argument soundness (validity plus true premises).

annotations.label: Complex argument

anchor.snippet: A complex argument is made up of a chain of simple arguments

annotations.note: No Mathlib object represents chained arguments.

annotations.label: Deductively valid argument

anchor.snippet: A deductively valid argument is one whose premises guarantee the truth of its conclusion

annotations.note: Semantic entailment T ⊨ φ expresses that premises guarantee the conclusion, but validity itself is not a packaged object.

annotations.label: Frogs-amphibians valid argument

anchor.snippet: all frogs are amphibians

annotations.note: A natural-language example of a valid inference, not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Tarski's three features of deduction

anchor.snippet: deductive arguments have three essential features

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Tarski's axioms for a consequence relation.

annotations.label: Modus ponens form

anchor.snippet: The modus ponens is a prominent rule of inference

annotations.note: The modus ponens schema is realized by Lean term application, not a named Mathlib rule.

annotations.label: Deductive inferences are truth-preserving

anchor.snippet: deductively valid inferences are truth-preserving

annotations.note: Truth-preservation is built into the definition of ⊨ (truth in all models) rather than stated as a separate theorem.

annotations.label: Ampliative arguments

anchor.snippet: Ampliative arguments are arguments whose conclusions contain additional information not found in their premises

annotations.note: Ampliative reasoning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Induction as statistical generalization

anchor.snippet: induction is often defined as a form of statistical generalization

annotations.note: This is inductive (non-deductive) reasoning, unrelated to Mathlib's mathematical induction.

annotations.label: Elephants are gray induction

anchor.snippet: all elephants are gray

annotations.note: A natural-language inductive-generalization example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Abduction

anchor.snippet: abduction is also called the inference to the best explanation

annotations.note: Abductive inference is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Breadcrumbs abduction

anchor.snippet: there is a plate with breadcrumbs in the kitchen in the early morning

annotations.note: A natural-language abduction example, not a formal object.

annotations.label: Fallacies

anchor.snippet: they are usually referred to as fallacies

annotations.note: Fallacies are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sunny-spiders fallacy

anchor.snippet: it is sunny today; therefore spiders have eight legs

annotations.note: A natural-language fallacy example with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Formal vs informal fallacies

anchor.snippet: Fallacies are usually divided into formal and informal fallacies

annotations.note: The taxonomy of fallacies is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Denying the antecedent

anchor.snippet: denying the antecedent is one type of formal fallacy

annotations.note: This formal fallacy schema has no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Definitory rules

anchor.snippet: They are definitory rules, which determine whether an inference is correct

annotations.note: The pragmatic notion of definitory rules is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strategic rules

anchor.snippet: Strategic rules specify which inferential moves are necessary to reach a given conclusion

annotations.note: Strategic/heuristic rules of inference are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formal system

anchor.snippet: A formal system of logic consists of a formal language together with a set of axioms and a proof system

annotations.note: A Language with a Theory (set of axiom sentences) is present, though Mathlib lacks a syntactic proof system component.

annotations.label: Axioms

anchor.snippet: In logic, axioms are statements that are accepted without proof

annotations.note: A first-order Theory is exactly a set of sentences taken as axioms.

annotations.label: Formal language

anchor.snippet: A formal language consists of an alphabet and syntactic rules

annotations.note: A first-order Language provides a signature/alphabet whose syntactic rules build terms and formulas.

annotations.label: Proof system

anchor.snippet: A proof system is a collection of rules to construct formal proofs

annotations.note: Mathlib's model theory is semantic and has no syntactic proof calculus/derivation system.

annotations.label: Conjunction introduction rule

anchor.snippet: the classical rule of conjunction introduction states that

annotations.note: Conjunction introduction is core Lean's `And.intro`/anonymous constructor, not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Semantics

anchor.snippet: A semantics is a system for mapping expressions of a formal language to their denotations

annotations.note: A Structure interprets a language's symbols and (via Realize) gives formulas their denotations, i.e. first-order semantics.

annotations.label: Soundness

anchor.snippet: A system of logic is sound when its proof system cannot derive a conclusion

annotations.note: Without a syntactic proof system, Mathlib states no soundness theorem relating derivability to entailment.

annotations.label: Completeness

anchor.snippet: A system is complete when its proof system can derive every conclusion that is semantically entailed by its premises

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes completeness of a theory (decides every sentence) but not Gödel proof-system completeness, since it has no proof calculus.

annotations.label: Systems of logic

anchor.snippet: Systems of logic are theoretical frameworks for assessing the correctness of reasoning and arguments

annotations.note: A meta-level survey term with no single Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Syllogism

anchor.snippet: A syllogism is a form of argument involving three propositions: two premises and a conclusion

annotations.note: Aristotelian syllogistic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Socrates is wise proposition

anchor.snippet: Socrates is wise

annotations.note: A natural-language example proposition, not a formal object.

annotations.label: Aristotelian subject types

anchor.snippet: the subject can be universal , particular , indefinite , or singular

annotations.note: Aristotelian classification of subject terms is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Valid Socrates syllogism

anchor.snippet: all men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal

annotations.note: A natural-language valid-syllogism example with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Invalid Socrates syllogism

anchor.snippet: all cats are mortal; Socrates is mortal; therefore Socrates is a cat

annotations.note: A natural-language invalid-syllogism (fallacy) example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Classical logic

anchor.snippet: Classical logic is distinct from traditional or Aristotelian logic

annotations.note: Mathlib's reasoning is classical (excluded middle and choice available) but 'classical logic' is not itself a defined object.

annotations.label: Propositional logic

anchor.snippet: Propositional logic comprises formal systems in which formulae are built from atomic propositions using logical connectives

annotations.note: Mathlib has no syntactic propositional-formula type, but Boolean algebra models classical propositional connectives algebraically.

annotations.label: First-order logic

anchor.snippet: First-order logic includes the same propositional connectives as propositional logic but differs from it because it articulates the internal structure of propositions

annotations.note: Mathlib fully formalizes first-order syntax (terms, predicates, quantifiers, connectives) via BoundedFormula.

annotations.label: This raven is black

anchor.snippet: this raven is black

annotations.note: A natural-language atomic-sentence example, not a formal declaration.

annotations.label: Extended logics

anchor.snippet: Extended logics are logical systems that accept the basic principles of classical logic

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the category of extended logics.

annotations.label: Modal logic

anchor.snippet: Modal logic is an extension of classical logic

annotations.note: No modal logic (necessity/possibility operators) is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Socrates is a banker

anchor.snippet: Socrates is a banker

annotations.note: A natural-language example sentence, not a formal object.

annotations.label: Deontic logic

anchor.snippet: deontic logic concerns the field of ethics and introduces symbols to express the ideas of obligation and permission

annotations.note: Deontic modal logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Epistemic modal logic

anchor.snippet: epistemic modal logic is used to represent the ideas of knowing something

annotations.note: Epistemic modal logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Higher-order logic

anchor.snippet: Higher-order logics extend classical logic not by using modal operators but by introducing new forms of quantification

annotations.note: Mathlib's ModelTheory is first-order only; higher-order logic is not formalized as an object (Lean's metatheory aside).

annotations.label: Some apples are sweet

anchor.snippet: some apples are sweet

annotations.note: A natural-language quantified example, not a formal declaration.

annotations.label: Deviant logics

anchor.snippet: Deviant logics are logical systems that reject some of the basic intuitions of classical logic

annotations.note: The category 'deviant logics' has no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Intuitionistic logic

anchor.snippet: Intuitionistic logic is a restricted version of classical logic

annotations.note: Heyting algebras are the algebraic semantics of intuitionistic propositional logic, though no proof system is formalized.

annotations.label: Law of excluded middle

anchor.snippet: It states that for every sentence, either it or its negation is true

annotations.note: `em : ∀ p, p ∨ ¬p` states exactly the law of excluded middle.

annotations.label: Multi-valued logics

anchor.snippet: Multi-valued logics depart from classicality by rejecting the principle of bivalence

annotations.note: Multi-valued logics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fuzzy logics

anchor.snippet: Fuzzy logics are multivalued logics that have an infinite number of

annotations.note: Fuzzy logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Paraconsistent logics

anchor.snippet: Paraconsistent logics are logical systems that can deal with contradictions

annotations.note: Paraconsistent logics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dialetheism

anchor.snippet: dialetheism , the view that contradictions are real

annotations.note: A philosophical position about contradictions, not a mathematical concept.

annotations.label: Pragmatic/dialogical approach

anchor.snippet: The pragmatic or dialogical approach to informal logic sees arguments as speech acts

annotations.note: A theory of argumentation as speech acts, outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Epistemic approach

anchor.snippet: The epistemic approach to informal logic, on the other hand, focuses on the epistemic role of arguments

annotations.note: An epistemological account of arguments, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Credence

anchor.snippet: the so-called credence

annotations.note: The Bayesian notion of credence is not formalized as such in Mathlib's probability library.

annotations.label: Philosophy of logic

anchor.section: Philosophy of logic and philosophical logic
anchor.snippet: Philosophy of logic is the philosophical discipline studying the scope and nature of logic

annotations.note: A philosophical discipline with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Philosophical logic

anchor.section: Philosophy of logic and philosophical logic
anchor.snippet: Philosophical logic is one of the areas within the philosophy of logic

annotations.note: A subfield of philosophy, outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Metalogic

anchor.snippet: Metalogic is the field of inquiry studying the properties of formal logical systems

annotations.note: Mathlib proves metalogical results (e.g. the compactness theorem) but does not formalize 'metalogic' as a field.

annotations.label: Mathematical logic

anchor.snippet: it refers to the study of logic within mathematics

annotations.note: Mathlib instantiates mathematical logic (model theory, computability) but does not define the field as a concept.

annotations.label: Computability theory

anchor.snippet: Computability theory is the branch of mathematical logic that studies effective procedures to solve calculation problems

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes core computability theory (Computable/Partrec functions, Turing machines), though not the field-level description.

annotations.label: Computational logic

anchor.snippet: Computational logic is the branch of logic and computer science that studies how to implement mathematical reasoning

annotations.note: A disciplinary description with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Formal semantics

anchor.snippet: Formal semantics is a subfield of logic, linguistics , and the philosophy of language

annotations.note: Formal semantics of natural language is a linguistics subfield not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principle of compositionality

anchor.snippet: It states that the meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its parts

annotations.note: The linguistic compositionality principle is not stated as a Mathlib theorem (though Realize is defined recursively on subformulas).

annotations.label: Epistemology of logic

anchor.snippet: The epistemology of logic studies how one knows that an argument is valid

annotations.note: An epistemological inquiry with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Principle of distributivity

anchor.snippet: the principle of distributivity in classical logic

annotations.note: `inf_sup_left` (a ⊓ (b ⊔ c) = a ⊓ b ⊔ a ⊓ c) generalizes the classical ∧/∨ distributivity law.

annotations.label: White horse paradox

anchor.snippet: which defends the thesis that a white horse is not a horse

annotations.note: A historical philosophical paradox with no Mathlib counterpart.
